agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Integration between Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) to enable real-time stock availability checks at order placement time. This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling request that fundamentally changes the autonomy of the Sales context.",
    "bounded_context": "Cross-cutting: Warehouse Management ↔ Sales/Order Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "StockLevel", "definition": "The current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management). Integer >= 0." },
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "A customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)." },
      { "term": "OrderLine", "definition": "A single line item within an Order, referencing a Product ID and desired Quantity." },
      { "term": "Product", "definition": "An item that exists in both contexts but may carry different meaning — in Context A it is a stockable unit, in Context B it is a sellable item referenced by ID." },
      { "term": "Stock Reservation", "definition": "A temporary claim against StockLevel that prevents other orders from consuming the same units. Does NOT yet exist in either context — must be introduced if blocking is implemented." },
      { "term": "Stock Available Indicator", "definition": "A read-model projection of approximate stock availability surfaced to the Sales context. Must be understood as eventually consistent, not authoritative." },
      { "term": "Blocked Order", "definition": "A new order state where the order is not confirmed because stock was deemed insufficient at placement time. Introduces a new lifecycle state into Context B." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "StockLevel for any Product must never go below zero.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Physical constraint — you cannot ship items that do not exist. If violated, the warehouse state is corrupt and downstream fulfillment will fail.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: For every Product, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, including under concurrent order placement."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The Warehouse Management context is the sole authority on actual stock quantities. No other context may write to or authoritatively determine StockLevel.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Bounded context ownership. If Sales can modify or override stock figures, data integrity across the system collapses.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: Only Warehouse Management domain events (ItemsPicked, ItemsReceived) mutate StockLevel. No direct writes from Sales."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An Order being 'blocked' due to insufficient stock is a Sales-context concern. The blocking decision lives in Context B, informed by data from Context A — not enforced by Context A.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy. The sales team may later decide to allow backorders, partial fulfillment, or override blocks for VIP customers. A VP could change this rule.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When an Order is placed and the stock availability projection for any OrderLine's Product is less than the requested Quantity, the Order transitions to 'Blocked' state."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The 'stock available' indicator shown in Sales is eventually consistent — it is a read-model projection, not a transactional guarantee. It must never be presented as a real-time guarantee to the user.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Cross-context data is inherently eventually consistent. Treating it as authoritative would create false guarantees, leading to overselling or phantom blocking. The system's integrity depends on acknowledging this boundary.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: The stock indicator in Sales is updated asynchronously from Warehouse events. ASSERT: The UI labels availability as approximate (e.g., 'approximately X in stock') not 'exactly X available'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "If stock-based blocking is implemented, a reservation mechanism must exist in Context A to prevent double-selling between the availability check and the actual pick.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without reservations, two concurrent orders could each see StockLevel=1, both pass the check, and then picking would violate BR-1. This is a concurrency invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When two Orders for the same Product with StockLevel=1 are placed concurrently, at most one is confirmed; the other is blocked or queued."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL). Unclaimed reservations expire and return stock to the available pool.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The TTL duration is a policy decision (5 minutes? 30 minutes?). But the existence of expiry prevents indefinite stock lockup, which is an operational concern, not a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: A reservation that is not fulfilled within the configured TTL is automatically released and StockLevel is restored."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The existing behavior — orders placed without checking Context A — must remain possible as a fallback. The system must not become unavailable for order placement if the Warehouse context is down.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Business continuity policy. Coupling order placement to warehouse availability creates a single point of failure. The business may choose to accept orders optimistically when stock data is unavailable.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: If the stock availability service is unreachable, orders are placed in a 'Pending Verification' state rather than rejected outright."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel = 10 in Context A",
      "when": "A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity = 5 of that Product",
      "then": "The Order is confirmed, and a reservation for 5 units is created in Context A, reducing available stock to 5"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel = 3 in Context A",
      "when": "A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity = 5 of that Product",
      "then": "The Order is blocked with a clear reason ('Insufficient stock for Product X: requested 5, available 3'), and no reservation is created"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "Two customers simultaneously place Orders each requesting Quantity = 1 of a Product with StockLevel = 1",
      "when": "Both orders are processed",
      "then": "Exactly one Order is confirmed with a reservation; the other is blocked. StockLevel never goes below 0."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A confirmed Order with an active reservation",
      "when": "The reservation TTL expires without the order being fulfilled (picked)",
      "then": "The reservation is released, available stock is restored, and the Order transitions to a review state"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "The Warehouse Management service is unavailable",
      "when": "A customer places an Order",
      "then": "The Order is accepted in a 'Pending Verification' state and queued for stock check when the service recovers. The customer is not blocked from ordering."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A sales team member viewing the order management UI",
      "when": "They look at a Product's availability indicator",
      "then": "They see an approximately-current stock figure clearly labeled as an estimate (e.g., '~10 in stock'), updated within a defined SLA (e.g., < 5 seconds of staleness under normal conditions)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "An Order with multiple OrderLines",
      "when": "Stock is sufficient for some lines but insufficient for others",
      "then": "The business must define the policy: block the entire order, or allow partial confirmation. This is an unresolved ambiguity (see escalations)."
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Stock is exactly equal to requested quantity (StockLevel = 5, Quantity = 5)", "expected_behavior": "Order is confirmed and reservation consumes all available stock. Subsequent orders for this product are blocked until stock is replenished or reservation expires." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple OrderLines in a single Order reference the same Product", "expected_behavior": "Quantities must be summed before checking availability. E.g., two lines of 3 each = 6 total required." },
    { "scenario": "Stock is replenished (ItemsReceived) while an Order is in Blocked state", "expected_behavior": "The system should re-evaluate blocked orders or notify the sales team. Auto-unblocking is a policy decision." },
    { "scenario": "A reservation is created but the Order is subsequently cancelled by the customer", "expected_behavior": "The reservation must be released immediately, restoring available stock." },
    { "scenario": "Race condition: stock check passes, but by the time reservation is created, stock has been consumed by another process", "expected_behavior": "The reservation creation must be atomic with the stock decrement. If it fails, the Order is blocked — never oversold." },
    { "scenario": "Product exists in Sales (Context B) but not yet in Warehouse (Context A)", "expected_behavior": "Stock availability is unknown, not zero. The system must distinguish between 'no stock data' and 'zero stock'. This should be surfaced as a data integrity issue, not silently block the order." },
    { "scenario": "Negative quantity on an OrderLine (data entry error)", "expected_behavior": "Rejected at the Sales context boundary. Quantity must be > 0. This is an input validation rule, not a stock rule." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling costs (refunds, expedited shipping, customer compensation) but introduces risk of lost sales if stock data is stale or overly conservative. Reservation TTLs that are too long lock up inventory; too short creates friction for slow-completing customers.",
    "operational": "Introduces tight coupling between two previously independent contexts. Warehouse Management now sits on the critical path for order placement — any latency or downtime in Context A directly impacts sales throughput. Requires monitoring, SLAs, and a fallback strategy. Reservation management adds operational complexity (expiry jobs, reconciliation).",
    "user": "Sales team gains visibility into stock, reducing manual checks and failed fulfillments. Customers may experience order rejection at placement time (friction) versus the current post-placement notification. The 'real-time' indicator creates an expectation of accuracy that eventual consistency may not fully deliver — manage expectations carefully.",
    "risk": "HIGH — This is a coupling decision that is easy to implement naively and extremely hard to undo. The primary risks are: (1) temporal coupling making order placement dependent on warehouse uptime, (2) stale stock data causing false blocks or false approvals, (3) reservation leaks permanently locking stock, (4) the word 'real-time' being interpreted as transactional consistency when it is actually eventual.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Orders may be blocked at placement time instead of accepted optimistically. Better experience when stock is genuinely unavailable (no false promises), worse when data is stale (unnecessary rejection)." },
      { "group": "Sales Team", "impact": "Gains stock visibility indicator — reduces manual lookups. Must understand it is approximate. New 'Blocked' order state requires new workflows and training." },
      { "group": "Warehouse Team", "impact": "New reservation concept introduces state they must be aware of. Reserved vs. available stock distinction affects pick planning. Reservation expiry creates reconciliation work." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Platform", "impact": "Must build and maintain the integration layer (events, read models, reservation service). Owns the SLA for stock data freshness. Must handle failure modes gracefully." },
      { "group": "Finance / Business", "impact": "Reduced overselling losses. Potential revenue impact from blocked orders that could have been fulfilled. Need data to tune the balance." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The feature request contains two fundamentally different concerns conflated into one: (1) a blocking mechanism (transactional, requires reservation) and (2) a 'real-time' display indicator (read model, eventually consistent). The spec does not distinguish between these, nor does it define critical policies: partial order handling, fallback behavior when warehouse is down, reservation TTL, or what 'real-time' means. The phrase 'automatically block' implies synchronous coupling but the phrase 'real-time indicator' implies async projection — these require very different architectures. Multiple ambiguities remain unresolved.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "StockLevel >= 0 is an inviolable invariant",
      "Warehouse Management is the sole authority on stock",
      "Cross-context data is eventually consistent, not transactionally consistent",
      "A reservation mechanism is required to prevent double-selling",
      "Fallback behavior is needed when the warehouse context is unavailable"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether partial order fulfillment is acceptable or the entire order must be blocked",
      "What 'real-time' means — sub-second? sub-minute? eventually consistent with a freshness SLA?",
      "Whether blocked orders should auto-unblock when stock is replenished",
      "Reservation TTL duration and what happens to the order when it expires",
      "Whether the business truly wants to couple order placement to warehouse availability (vs. optimistic acceptance with async verification)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The two bounded contexts communicate via domain events, not shared database",
      "The 'stock available indicator' is a read-model projection in Context B, not a direct query to Context A",
      "The business accepts that the indicator is approximate, not exact",
      "Orders currently have no 'Blocked' state — this is a new lifecycle state that must be introduced",
      "The integration does not change ownership of Product in either context"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'automatically block if stock is insufficient' but does not define behavior for multi-line orders where some lines have sufficient stock and others do not. Should the entire order be blocked, or should partial fulfillment be allowed?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales Team", "Customers", "Warehouse Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Block the entire order if any line has insufficient stock (simpler, but may frustrate customers who want available items)",
        "B: Allow partial confirmation — confirm available lines, block insufficient ones (complex, introduces split-order fulfillment)",
        "C: Block the entire order but suggest an alternative order with only available items"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Start with Option A (block entire order) as it preserves order atomicity and is far simpler to implement. Introduce partial fulfillment as a future enhancement once the blocking mechanism is proven."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The phrase 'real-time' for the stock indicator is undefined. Cross-bounded-context data is inherently eventually consistent. What freshness SLA does the sales team actually need?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales Team", "Engineering / Platform"],
      "options": [
        "A: Sub-second freshness via direct synchronous query to Context A (tight coupling, fragile)",
        "B: Event-driven projection with < 5 second typical staleness (loose coupling, resilient)",
        "C: Periodic polling with configurable interval (simplest, but potentially minutes stale)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — event-driven projection. It aligns with bounded context autonomy and provides acceptable freshness without creating temporal coupling. Label the indicator as approximate."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No fallback behavior is defined for when the Warehouse context is unavailable. Should order placement be blocked entirely (fail-closed) or proceed optimistically (fail-open)?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Finance / Business"],
      "options": [
        "A: Fail-closed — reject all orders when stock cannot be verified (safe but potentially catastrophic for revenue during outages)",
        "B: Fail-open — accept orders optimistically and verify stock asynchronously when service recovers (revenue-preserving but may oversell)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — fail-open with 'Pending Verification' state. This preserves the current behavior as a degraded mode and prevents a warehouse outage from halting all sales."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No reservation mechanism currently exists. Introducing one requires defining TTL, expiry behavior, and how expired reservations affect order state. None of this is specified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Warehouse Team", "Engineering / Platform", "Sales Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define reservation TTL as a configurable business parameter (e.g., 15 minutes default)",
        "B: No TTL — reservations persist until order is fulfilled or cancelled (risk of permanent stock lockup)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A with a 15-minute default TTL. Expired reservations release stock and move the order to a 'Reservation Expired' state for manual review."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
